TextENGNew: The lights of home blazed bright and dazzling.
That light suddenly reminded me I was hungry.
I looked at my watch—it was almost seven in the evening.
changeReason: New specifies 'watch' (wristwatch) instead of 'clock,' matching 腕時計; also adds 'in the evening' for 午後.

TextENGNew: I had been completely convinced it was two or three in the morning. I was amazed that my internal clock could fall this far off in a single night.
The living room window—warm light leaked through the gap in the curtains.
How different the world was on either side of that glass.
If that man had never appeared, I would surely be inside, in that light.
Still buzzing from the fun of the festival, I would surely have been in a good mood, telling my parents about the wonderful time I'd had with my friends.
And the warm dinner waiting for me—I would have eaten almost none of it, full from all the festival food, gone up to my room, and surrendered willingly to sleep, burrowing under the covers.
But where I was now was nothing like that.
The feeble light of the streetlamp held not a fragment of warmth.
I had no part in the warm family circle inside, no warm meal waiting for me, and no permission to give myself to drowsiness.
Exposed to the cold rain—I whipped my body forward, toward what still had to be done.
I noticed I was starting to feel sorry for myself.
changeReason: Old translates 卑屈 as 'timid, cowardly'; new translates as 'feel sorry for myself' (self-pitying), a different emotional state.

TextENGNew: Why did I have to go through something this painful—why did I have to kill someone?
I was almost losing sight of the reason.
I had chosen the fastest and most fundamental solution to save Satoko—and I had carried it out without hesitation.
That man was dead.
Gone.
He would never hurt Satoko again.
A problem with Satoko that the rest of us had agonized over for days upon days, and ultimately declared unsolvable—I had resolved it in a single night.
Alone, without help from anyone.
Right now, Satoko might be preparing dinner for an uncle who might never come home, trembling over whether she would be screamed at for the taste.
changeReason: Old says Satoko doesn't know 'when' uncle returns (implying he will); new says 'might never come home,' reflecting narrator's knowledge the uncle is dead.

TextENGNew: But her uncle would never come home again.
She would never have to cower from his voice, never be made to cry by it again.
changeReason: New translates 悲しまされる as 'made to cry' rather than old's 'feel sad'; changes 怒声に怯える to 'cower from his voice' rather than 'fear his wrath.'

TextENGNew: I... ah... I did it.
I saved Satoko. I actually did it.
I had been so focused on nothing but killing that man that the reality of having saved Satoko hadn't registered.
``...Oh. ...Aha, haha... hahahaha...``
changeReason: Old translates そっか as 'I did'; new correctly renders it as 'Oh' (realization), matching the Japanese interjection.

TextENGNew: I did it. I actually did it.
changeReason: Old mistranslates やったんだ。やりとげたんだ ('I did it, I accomplished it') as 'I did something good'; new correctly conveys the meaning.

TextENGNew: That's right—I finally freed Satoko.
changeReason: Old omits the explicit mention of Satoko (沙都子を解放してやれた = freed Satoko) and paraphrases as 'did something good'; new accurately renders the Japanese.

TextENGNew: Hot tears rolled down my face before I knew it—and I understood at last what had been powering my exhaustion all this time. It was guilt.
The blade of guilt had been pricking me this whole time—not stabbing deep, just grazing the surface, over and over—quietly wounding my heart.
It hurt so much I felt it throbbing.
I cried because it hurt, because it was painful.
I cried because I was glad I had saved Satoko.
I cried because I didn't understand anything anymore. Just cried and cried.
After holding those hot tears in my mouth for a while—my heart eased, just a little.
Come on, Keiichi Maebara. That's enough. There's nothing to be ashamed of. Nothing to regret.
Right now, body and soul, every part of me is exhausted. It's natural to feel nothing.
Tomorrow, and the day after—for a while, I might not feel anything at all.
But there is one thing that is true.
I saved Satoko. That is a fact that cannot be taken from me.
A day will come when it is repaid.
changeReason: Old uses 'we would surely be rewarded' (adding plural subject not in Japanese); new uses 'it is repaid,' closer to the passive 報われる.

TextENGNew: A day will come when I can be proud of the great and difficult thing I accomplished—to bring Satoko's smile back.
To reach that day—I have to get through tonight perfectly.
If I let anything slip now, all today's suffering turns to nothing.
I lifted my face from where it had been bowed, and looked up at the rainy sky with no sign of stopping.
I squeezed my eyes shut and summoned strength from the bottom of my body one more time.
changeReason: Old says 'rubbed my eyes'; new says 'squeezed my eyes shut,' correctly translating 両目を固くつぶって (squeezed both eyes tightly shut).

TextENGNew: How long have I been standing here in a daze?
This is no time for this. Even now, someone might be finding the body.
Even that negative thought was proof that a normal human fear had come back to life in me.
I let that fear push me forward and went into the shed to look for the lantern and the second shovel.
It was fortunate that I had memorized the features of the streetlamp near the body.
In this total darkness, there was a real chance I wouldn't have been able to find even a body.
When I left, I hadn't noticed that—I had no intention of memorizing this spot.
Which is why managing to return to the body safely was genuinely lucky.
The area around the body had become one large muddy pool.
The body itself was half submerged in it.
I noticed later that there was a drainage ditch nearby, long unused and blocked with leaves and earth, and rainwater had been overflowing it.
My shoes had been full of water long since, so I stepped straight into the mud pool without any hesitation.
The muddy torrent was washing everything away.
Blood traces and the like—they wouldn't remain. Truly the rain had been a blessing from heaven.
changeReason: Old attributes action to the narrator ('I couldn't leave any traces') while JPN describes traces not remaining due to rain (残るまい = traces won't remain); new correctly attributes it to the rain.

TextENGNew: I checked the surroundings, then turned off the lantern to be safe.
The world went pitch black—but after a while my eyes adjusted, and I found I could just make out shapes by the faint light that somehow reached from the distant streetlamp.
changeReason: Old says narrator 'reached the streetlight'; new correctly says light 'reached from the distant streetlamp' to the narrator's position, matching the Japanese.

TextENGNew: I drove the shovel into the mud right next to the body.
The ground had softened beyond what I expected, and the blade sank in with ease.
I had to dig fast. If I took too long, my will might fail again.
It felt like digging a hole on a beach.
Digging was easy enough—but the endless flow of mud kept filling in what I'd dug.
It wasn't going the way I wanted.
No matter how much I dug, the water filled it back in, so I had no real sense of how deep it was. The only way to measure was by how far my own feet were sinking into the muck.
I'm really digging here.
It's fine. Don't give up. Dig—dig—dig.
Occasionally, a car passed on the road, and I scrambled to hide in the shadow of the trees more than once.
Everyone just passed through. No one was straining to see into this darkness.
I knew that in my head—and still couldn't help hiding.
What truly frightened me wasn't so much the chance that one of those cars might glimpse me—it was that the distance at which I could sense them coming was growing shorter.
At the start, I could hear the sound of tires cutting through puddles from far, far away.
Then I stopped noticing in time. For the last car, I didn't sense it until the headlights were already bleeding through.
I could feel my own senses going dull.
What if someone quietly crept up to watch me—here, in this most damning of scenes, burying a body—and I didn't even notice?
At that thought, I snapped my head around and scanned in every direction.
I woke every nerve I had been letting drowse—scanning the surrounding darkness for any sign of life.
And if I found that someone was hiding in the trees and watching me—?
Predator's instinct—baring its fangs with a cold gleam.
changeReason: Old says the feeling 'snarled at me' (hostile toward narrator); new renders it as the narrator's own predatory instinct 'baring its fangs,' matching the Japanese.

TextENGNew: ...If they were there—I would dig one more hole.
I had come this far.
To make it perfect—there was no payment I feared making anymore.
I don't know what expression I had on my face at that moment—but it must have been blazing with something.
Nothing. Not a trace of anyone. Every one of my five senses confirmed it.
I did not breathe a sigh of relief.
How long did I keep digging, scooping out the mud?
The hole had reached approximately knee depth.
This might be enough. As a test—I could try rolling the body in.
"...Hah... hah... hmm."
I swallowed the ragged breath and steadied myself.
For all that I had beaten him so thoroughly, grabbing the body's hands was frightening.
changeReason: Old says 'afraid of touching the corpse' (general); new specifies 'grabbing the body's hands,' matching 死体の手を取る.

TextENGNew: So I grabbed the legs and dragged instead.
It didn't budge with anything less than full effort.
That was to be expected. One look at the man's build—he had to be well over eighty kilograms.
I put everything I had into dragging him.
By this point, any groundless fear that he might suddenly move had long since faded.
The uncle's body sank perfectly into the muddy water. The depth was more than sufficient.
changeReason: Old uses 'His body'; new specifies 'The uncle's body,' matching 叔父の死体.

TextENGNew: All right—bury him. Quickly, quickly, quickly.
Once this was covered over, it would all be finished.
Buried, hidden—as if none of it had ever happened!
Knowing that it was almost over drove me into a senseless panic—as if time itself were chasing me.
As if I were possessed by the delusion that the body might come back to life if I didn't cover it fast enough.
I shoveled mud and earth into the hole in a frantic rush.
If I dawdled, someone might see me here.
A moment ago I had thought, if someone sees me I'll just kill them too—but not anymore. Now I was seized by an absolute terror that if anyone saw me, everything would be over.
By the end, it was a total mess.
Splashing and churning.
I dragged and raked the shovel all over the ground trying to flatten the surface.
I was covered in mud, head to toe.
The rain washed the mud from my skin but not from my blackened clothes.
Frantic and terrified, soaked with rain and sweat and mud—my body and soul were both mired in it.
"...Hah... hah... HAH!!"
I threw down the shovel and sat down in the mud, breathing hard.
I was so completely drained I wanted to fall over and sleep right there.
It was finished.
This time—it was really, truly—finished!
That man was now at the bottom of the mud.
The ground bore the traces of digging now, but this rain and the relentless muddy flow would soon cover everything over.
Yes. It was over.
"I did it—I did it—I actually did it, you bastard—!!"
Then I fell backward and lay face-up in the mud.
Raindrops hammered my face without mercy, but I didn't much mind.
I lay there for a while, blank—then pulled myself upright.
It was over.
So I could go home.
I had buried everything.
So it had become as if it never happened.
And if it had never happened—there was no reason for me to keep standing here being rained on.
I got to my feet slowly.
I wouldn't need the shovel anymore.
I tried twisting it to collapse it, but my exhausted body didn't have the strength.
Fine—I didn't need to fold it. I dragged the shovel behind me and walked toward the light.
I picked up the bicycle I had left lying in the roadside brush.
The unfolded shovel wouldn't fit into the front basket no matter how I tried.
As I had done with the bat, I shoved it down the back of my shirt.
Unlike the bat, its corners dug in and hurt.
And the moment my posture relaxed even slightly, it slipped out from behind my back.
In the end, I had no choice but to hold the shovel in one hand and ride the bicycle with the other.
Utterly exhausted, riding one-handed in a downpour—I wobbled along like a drunk.
But it didn't matter. Wobbly or not, every pedal brought me closer to home.
Ahead, two wavering lights appeared.
A car coming. Bad—I had to get out of the way.
From deep in the darkness, a car took shape.
The blinding headlights caught me.
A short, sharp honk told me I was in the middle of the road.
I had to move.
I knew that—but riding one-handed while utterly spent, there was nothing I could do.
I wobbled left, wobbled right—serpentining without committing to either side.
The car had assumed I would swerve at the last moment and wasn't slowing much, bearing down on me fast.
Its silhouette loomed. Thinking I had to dodge—I wrenched the handlebar hard—lost my balance—and toppled over.
My whole body went flying, one foot caught in the frame.
This—is really bad!
The sleepy numbness was shocked away and cold sweat burst from every pore—
—the car ground its wheels with a screech, cutting hard to the left, spinning half a turn—and stopped, barely in time.
The driver's door opened and the driver got out.
I expected to be yelled at—what the hell are you doing in a place like this?
"My, my. ...Who do we have here."
A woman's voice. One I recognized. I pushed myself up from where I had fallen face-down and looked.
"...T-... Takano-san."
"Good evening. A beautiful moonlit night, isn't it? ...Keiichi-kun."
There was no moon visible anywhere, in this downpour.
Takano-san said that and smiled, unfolding a small umbrella.
That smile—for some reason it looked like it could see straight through me, and it made my skin crawl.
Of all the people I knew, this was the one I least wanted to run into.
"What are you doing out at this hour? No umbrella, soaked to the bone and covered in mud. ...And what is that? ...A shovel?"
She had just arrived by car.
There was no way she could know what I had been doing. And yet—she smiled as if she already knew everything.
Calm down, Keiichi Maebara.
This woman always smiled in this cryptic, unsettling way. That was just how she was.
Be careful not to say something incriminating by second-guessing yourself.
"...The rumor is you showed up to a baseball game with a golf club. So the shovel—is that the same sort of thing? A substitute for an umbrella, perhaps? Heeheehee."
Better play this down. If I could just get through this moment, everything would be fine.
"...When I went treasure-hunting at the dam site with Rena, I... left this behind."
"...Oh? And you went to retrieve it in this downpour?"
"...It wasn't raining when I left the house."
"But it started quite a while ago? You've been outside for a good long time."
"...Well, I was wandering around, so..."
"Even so. You came from over there, didn't you? That's the completely wrong direction from the dam. ...Heeheehee."
Takano-san clearly understood that something extremely irregular was going on with me.
And she was toying with me—playing games with words, backing me into a corner.
She might already have a faint suspicion of what I had been doing.
Of all the people to run into, and at the worst possible moment.
I could only curse my rotten luck.
I had worked so hard to get here—one more step and everything would have been over—so why, at the very last moment, of all people—!
What do you do, Keiichi Maebara?
The shovel, a weapon, was still in my hand.
Should I... silence Takano-san?
Even if killing Takano-san would be easy—dealing with the car she had driven here would be much harder.
I couldn't drive a car. It wasn't like a motorcycle.
But even if the car remained—as long as there was no proof that I had killed her...
That's right—a chance encounter in the middle of the night, in a downpour, with no planning at all.
Unless I dropped a handkerchief with my initials, how would anyone know it was me?
In the core of my body—a dark flame tried to rise, quietly.
"...What a frightening look. Have I been teasing you too hard? Heehee!"
Takano-san laughed on her own and brought the conversation to a close.
But the dark clouds in my heart did not clear.
"That aside—how long are you going to stay sprawled there with your bicycle on top of you? Do you think you could move?"
If I moved, she would leave.
I thought so, and started to push the bicycle aside and get up—but my foot twisted with a crack and I fell again.
"Ow—it hurts, it hurts, it hurts, it HURTS—!! ...Ughhh!!"
Sharp pain from the twisted ankle. What an idiot. Of all the times—a sprain?
"Oh... are you all right? Did you twist it?"
Takano-san crouched down and peered at the ankle I kept rubbing.
She seemed to be comparing my expression with the injury, gauging how serious it was.
"Can you stand?"
There was no way I could stand up.
All I could do was crawl on the ground like a worm.
Takano-san glanced at her watch and then at her car, thought for a moment, and opened the passenger door.
Then—she offered me her shoulder.
"I'll take you home. It feels like I ran you over, which is uncomfortable."
"...Ugh—th, thank you..."
"Can't very well abandon you out here. You're lucky I'm such a kind person, heeheeheehee."
Anyone who called themselves kind was never to be trusted—that's what I thought, but borrowing her shoulder, I could hardly say that.
With Takano-san's help, I settled into the passenger seat.
She turned around and looked at my bicycle, still lying where it had fallen.
"...Can the bicycle wait until later?"
Her eyes made it abundantly clear: it was too much trouble, and too heavy, and she didn't want mud in her car.
But the bicycle had my name written on it. There was no way I could leave it here.
"Um—could we... maybe find a way to fit it in the car? I kind of... need it."
"The trunk is already full. Putting it in the back seat will get mud all over. Though I suppose you've already gotten the seat quite dirty enough just by getting in."
I looked into the back seat—there was already a folding bicycle in there, belonging to someone.
There looked to be just enough room to fit mine too.
That thought must have shown plainly in my eyes, because Takano-san seemed to read it straight from my face.
"...All right, all right. I'll put it in. Just for tonight, I'll be kind to you. ...Heeheeheehee."
Complaining the whole time, Takano-san picked up my bicycle and crammed it into the back seat.
If she was going to complain, that was one thing—but did she have to keep laughing that unsettling laugh at the same time?
And even more than the bicycle—there was something I didn't want to leave here.
The shovel could become the key that connected the buried body directly to me.
"Um, I'm sorry to ask, but... could you also bring the shovel?"
Takano-san's expression was unmistakably puzzled.
"...A shovel? ...Fine. ...Is it important to you?"
The more I spoke, the more disadvantaged I'd become.
Something told me that, and I avoided forcing an answer.
My silence was apparently too much for her to bear—Takano-san shrugged her shoulders in a resigned sigh.
"...All right, fine. You can't bear to be without your little shovel, can you? No need to give me that scary look."
Takano-san brought the shovel that had been lying in the road.
I clutched it like I would never let go. Takano-san watched this with an expression of clear puzzlement.
"Well, let's go. Your house—is it the Maebara Mansion?"
"Why does everyone call it that?"
"Because it is a mansion. I envy people who have money."
Talking like this, Takano-san released the handbrake and pressed the accelerator.
She executed a smooth U-turn using the shoulder and rapidly picked up speed.
In the rearview mirror, the cursed place dissolved and vanished into the dark behind us.
"...Were you able to?"
"What? ...Sorry, I didn't quite catch that. What did you say just now?"
Takano-san had said something suddenly, and I'd been too distracted to catch it.
Takano-san kept her eyes on the road ahead and repeated herself.
"The body."
"Did you bury it well?"
I didn't react with a dramatic lurch of the heart.
Instead—my lungs simply constricted, and I could not draw a breath.
"Wh—what are you... saying..."
"When burying a body in the mountains, you know. If you don't dig quite deep, stray dogs will often catch the scent and dig it back up."
"A stray dog carries home a human bone, the case is blown open, police conduct a mountain search... that's not such an uncommon story, you know? ...Heehee."
Clanging—booming—
Multiple rusted bells rang and rang inside my skull.
Had I buried it deep enough—deep enough to say with absolute certainty it was safe?
The answer was no.
In my panic to finish and have it all be over, I couldn't rule out that I had buried it carelessly and shallowly.
No no no no—never mind that—more than that—
This woman—how does she know?!
She happened to drive past that area.
She happened to run into me. That was all.
It was unthinkable by any ordinary reasoning that she had watched everything from the shadows of the trees and then doubled back with her car. And yet—how—
Keiichi Maebara. You still have to kill her. No other choice.
My whole body snapped back to alertness. I felt my pupils dilate.
The car was too narrow to swing the shovel at her.
Then—with these hands—strangle her—
But she was driving right now. If I choked her now—there could be an accident—
Just then, Takano-san suddenly glanced at me and met my eyes.
"...No comeback?"
I didn't understand what she was talking about for a moment and was caught off guard.
"You and I don't seem to be a very good match."
Takano-san said that with a look of boredom and returned her gaze to the road.
I replayed what she had said again and again—and finally realized she had meant it as a terrible joke.
Whether it was really only a joke, I didn't know.
No lie I could tell would fool a woman like this.
Right now, while she was driving—I couldn't kill her.
And once we arrived at my house, killing her would be even harder to do.
The very first moment—that first instant we met—had been the best chance.
Letting it slip by had been a big, painful mistake.
After that—Takano-san said nothing more.
Silence filled the car, suffocating.
What kind of woman had I gotten involved with?
After so much had happened today, and it had somehow gone well enough, and all that remained was to go home and sleep—at the absolute last moment, what rotten luck.
That anxiety was gradually displaced—by oncoming drowsiness—becoming, more and more, something that simply didn't matter.
...Is that all right? Keiichi Maebara?
...This woman—she would probably be better off not left alive.
...Right now—I'm tired.
The brakes engaged, and the seatbelt tightened against my chest.
That woke me.
I had apparently fallen asleep.
Outside the window, a faint glow outlined a large building.
It was dark, but I knew at once it was my own house.
"I'll go call your family. Can you manage alone for just a moment? Heeheeheehee."
"Don't treat me like a child. And look—I'm fine now. I can walk by myself."
"My, my. A moment ago you were making such a pained face and borrowing my shoulder? Perhaps you wanted to lean on a big sister figure? ...Heeheeheehee."
Saying that, Takano-san got out of the car and opened her umbrella.
The sound of rain on the umbrella was as fierce as ever.
I gingerly prodded the ankle I'd twisted earlier.
It still hurt and felt wrong—but it wasn't as severe as before.
Rather than being relieved it wasn't a serious injury, I found myself regretting first that if I hadn't twisted it there—I might have had the option of striking Takano-san down right then.
Takano-san almost certainly, without any doubt, had concluded I was involved in some crime.
Knowing about the whole situation with Satoko, she might even be able to imagine who I had killed.
Click.
Takano-san opened the passenger door for me.
"Are you sure you don't need my shoulder? You can lean on me one more time if you like. Heehee."
I ignored her and got out on my own, letting that serve as my answer.
It was still raining fairly hard.
I unloaded my bicycle from the back seat.
I was briefly curious whose the other bicycle was, but it didn't matter now.
I walked the bicycle under Takano-san's umbrella toward the front door.
"...Thank you. This is fine, from here."
The ankle that had hurt so terribly before still ached a little, but wasn't nearly as bad as it had been—and that irritated me immensely.
Truly, truly—this had been a stroke of terrible luck.
"Well, I'll be off then. A late-night drive alone with Keiichi-kun—let's keep this our little secret? Heehee... especially from Jirou-san."
Jirou-san?
Oh—that must be Tomitake-san.
She calls Tomitake-san Jirou. His full name must be Jirou Tomitake.
Just then—the other folding bicycle in the back seat, which I'd paid no attention to, came back to me.
A day or two ago, when I was walking late to school past the dam construction site, Tomitake-san had come up to talk with me.
And the bicycle he had been riding at the time was—if I remembered correctly—
"That bicycle in the back—it's Tomitake-san's, isn't it?"
Takano-san's expression sharpened for just a moment.
"...Why would you think that?"
"I've seen it when we met before. The frame has a distinctive look."
"...Hmm. ...But isn't that strange? For this bicycle to be Jirou-san's?"
"Strange? ...Wait—what?"
Takano-san let her hair drift elegantly and looked straight into my eyes.
"Because—there are no inns in Hinamizawa, you see? So Jirou-san would be staying in town."
"...Is that so? ...Well—if that's the case, isn't it?"
"You can't walk between town and Hinamizawa—the distance is too great? There's no bus either. You'd need a bicycle at the very least."
She didn't need to tell me. That was why Tomitake-san rode a bicycle.
"So if Jirou-san's bicycle is in my car—and Jirou-san is not in my car—something is strange, isn't it? Jirou-san would be in Hinamizawa without his bicycle."
"Leaving Jirou-san behind and only taking his bicycle—doesn't that seem strange?"
Takano-san said this with hawk-sharp eyes—and I had to admit I wasn't entirely sure what was supposed to be strange about it.
"What's strange about that, exactly? You and Tomitake-san are close. Doesn't it make sense you might be carrying his bicycle for some reason?"
"That's not what I mean. What I mean is—in Hinamizawa, Jirou-san and his bicycle cannot exist separately. That's all."
"For that reason—there's no way this bicycle could be Jirou-san's. Does that make sense?"
Takano-san's phrasing was a little hard to follow—but beyond that, there was something unnerving about the quiet intensity it carried.
And yet—the most instinctive part of me warned that I should not pursue this further.
"That's my bicycle. Jirou-san helped me choose it, so the taste is similar—that much I'll grant you."
"...Oh—was that what it was? No wonder it looked so similar to—"
"You and I did not meet tonight."
The conversation made no sense.
"What are you talking about?"
"You and I did not meet tonight."
When I couldn't find any reply and just stood there, Takano-san smiled with that unsettling quality and repeated the short phrase one more time.
"You and I did not meet tonight."
"...You and I—tonight. Did not meet."
Something cold and deeply unpleasant ran up my spine.
I understood, because I had just taken a human life with my own hands.
Just as I had reached the conclusion that the only option was to strike this woman down and silence her without a second thought—this woman had arrived at the same conclusion, and I could feel it.
My instincts warned me again. I should never have met this woman tonight.
The smell of something rotten that drifted from her—it was certainly no different from what was seeping from me.
The particular aura carried by a human who had transcended ordinary life—who had no hesitation in taking lives for a purpose. Or perhaps—the urge to vomit was a better word for it.
That is to say—I could not explain it well—but Takano-san was the same as me.
That is to say—she was a person who should not be here.
A person who must be made to have not been here.
A person who wished to meet no one.
changeReason: Old reverses the subject: 'someone nobody would have wanted to see' (others avoid her) vs JPN 誰とも会うことを望まない (she doesn't want to meet anyone).

TextENGNew: And a person who was perfectly capable of eliminating whoever she had met—and making that meeting un-happen.
changeReason: Old omits the key meaning about eliminating witnesses; Japanese says she can eliminate whoever she meets and make the meeting un-happen, but old only says 'we could pretend we never met.'

TextENGNew: So. That meant—just as I no longer wanted anything touched—she, too, wanted nothing touched.
changeReason: Old translates 触れられたくない as 'wanted nothing to do with her'; new translates as 'wanted nothing touched,' better capturing the idea of not wanting anything disturbed.

TextENGNew: And she, too, wished that she had not met me.
Both of us were wishing for the same thing.
If we both wished for the same thing—Takano-san's proposal was entirely rational. And it was not one that should be refused.
"If that's agreeable to you, then that suits me as well. ...You and I did not meet tonight."
"That's right. For your sake too, that's better."
"Why do you say that?"
``You're so tiresome with your questions. Can't you work that out for yourself, boy?``
changeReason: Old translates いちいちうるさいわね as 'Are you seriously asking me that?'; new renders it as 'You're so tiresome with your questions,' better capturing Takano's irritation.

TextENGNew: That dismissive word—boy—cut off our conversation cleanly.
In front of a house, of all places—the least suitable location for a killing.
And yet—between the two of us, a mutual, freezing killing intent was seeping out.
I am going to be killed—right here, right now.
Ah—then I really should have attacked the moment we met.
Cold sweat oozed across my whole body.
Fine. Come if you want to. I'm ready, whenever you are.
When I made that resolve—
Takano-san swept her hair back elegantly—and turned on her heel.
"Lucky for you. That I'm a kind person."
What had been lucky, exactly?
Takano-san being a kind person—what had that changed? I didn't understand.
Takano-san walked back to her car. There was no parting farewell.
I had accomplished the great feat of killing Satoko's uncle—and still felt no sense of achievement.
On this sacred night of Oyashiro-sama's curse—when one life was permitted to be taken.
On this rain-drenched night, when the demon-possessed wandered as agents of Oyashiro-sama.
Two demons who should not have met—had met, by chance.
And the demons—went their separate ways, triumphant.
There was no need to fight.
Each had already achieved their purpose.
Through the car window with rain cascading down it, Takano-san and I made eye contact.
That female demon's lips curled in what looked like a knowing, brazen smile.
Too late to kill her now.
Too late. The very first moment—that first instant we met—had been the only chance.
changeReason: Old says the first moment was 'my best chance'; new says 'the only chance,' a meaningfully different assessment.

TextENGNew: That was what I understood now, clearly, in hindsight.
She should not have been let out alive.
One short honk—and Takano-san's car drove off into the downpour.
The only woman who knew the truth of tonight—vanishing into the darkness.
As long as that woman continued to live—tonight would never truly end.
I hoped she would have an accident.
In this downpour—slip on mud or something, hit a large tree head-on—dead.
This wasn't a delusion.
It was a wish—and yes—a command.
Die. Just die.
And keep your mouth shut forever.
Because right now, you're surely wishing for my death too.
My curse fell on deaf ears—Takano-san's tail-lights disappeared into the pitch-black night.
The car's lights were completely gone.
Swallowed into the sound of rain—not a sound or sign remained.
changeReason: Old specifies 'engine sound' fading; new renders 音も何ももうわからない as 'not a sound or sign remained,' which is more accurate.

TextENGNew: I too turned on my heel.
To draw the curtain on this mad night.
To open the front door and end tonight.
changeReason: Old says 'end it all' which could imply suicidal intent; new says 'end tonight,' accurately matching 今夜を終わらせる.

TextENGNew: I walked toward the front door.
My mud-soaked shoes made a pathetic squelching sound with every step. Squelch. Squelch.
Squelch. Squelch.
You're all right, Keiichi Maebara.
Squelch. Squelch.
That woman is going to die.
Squelch. Squelch.
An end fitting for that woman.
Wrap her in living flame—let her dance until she turns to black ash.
I'll burn you to nothing in the fires of hell—enough for what you deserved to be burned.
"That's what I'm wishing for. ...And I mean every word."
Squelch.
...Wait. Did I... just stop walking?
changeReason: Old states 'I hadn't taken a step' (factual); new questions 'Did I just stop walking?' — the Japanese え？今、俺。歩いてない expresses sudden confused realization.

TextENGNew: ...And yet—a footstep.
I turned around—without any particular feeling.
If there was one footstep that wasn't mine—someone else was standing behind me, and that would simply be the natural explanation.
changeReason: 'Footprint' vs 'footstep' is a translation difference but the logical conclusion (someone behind me) is the same.

TextENGNew: And if there was someone else standing there—?
I'd had enough.
Whoever it was—not tonight. Absolutely not tonight.
This time—I would kill them. This time without a moment's hesitation.
Without a shred of doubt—I would turn back, and dig one more hole.
But—fortunately—there was no one there. And so tonight, I didn't have to kill anyone else.
That night—I thought nothing more of it.
changeReason: Old says 'I couldn't handle any more of it' (emotional overload); new says 'I thought nothing more of it' (dismissed it), matching それ以上、気にならなかった.

TextENGNew: Even when I stopped and heard one footstep too many—I told myself I was tired, and left it at that.
